item.title: One pissed off Bartender
item.description: Credits: Pissedoffbartender https://www.tiktok.com/@pissedoffbartender/video/7455003582119546143?is_from_webapp=1&sender_device=mobile&sender_web_id=7455266389012579872 Here's the Linktree of PissedoffBartender Please, show him your appreciation. Thank you! https://linktr.ee/pissedoffbartender?utm_source=linktree_profile_share Also a big shoutout for @VeteranGridSquares who's a best friend of #PissedoffBartender. Show him some respect as well if you will. Thank you! https://imgur.com/gallery/bilBBj0/comment/2436181035
item.dc:creator: AdventureRoute69

item.title: Imgur loves books (version 2024)
item.description:
1 “Humanity has colonized the solar system—Mars, the Moon, the Asteroid Belt and beyond—but the stars are still out of our reach. “Jim Holden is XO of an ice miner making runs from the rings of Saturn to the mining stations of the Belt. When he and his crew stumble upon a derelict ship, the Scopuli, they find themselves in possession of a secret they never wanted. A secret that someone is willing to kill for—and kill on a scale unfathomable to Jim and his crew. War is brewing in the system unless he can find out who left the ship and why. “Detective Miller is looking for a girl. One girl in a system of billions, but her parents have money and money talks. When the trail leads him to the Scopuli and rebel sympathizer Holden, he realizes that this girl may be the key to everything. “Holden and Miller must thread the needle between the Earth government, the Outer Planet revolutionaries, and secretive corporations—and the odds are against them. But out in the Belt, the rules are different, and one small ship can change the fate of the universe.” This is a series that I started way back when only 4 books had been published, and I learned that there were going to be 9 in total planned (10 if you include the short story anthology). After blitzing the first 3, I figured it would be easier on me to just wait for them all to be done and then binge them in a single go than to have to wait a year, each year, for the next to come out. They stuck pretty close to their timetable, so that approach seemed to work out. In retrospect, it’s kind of like the timetable to a NASA project, “Yes, we’ve launched this vehicle into space and we’re stoked for the results, but it won’t get to its destination for another 8 years so we’ll have to be patient.” I read this series all the way through, and then at the end read their short story anthology. You can read it that way, or you can read each tale after its corresponding novel (probably best to check publication dates instead of trying to match internal timelines), which is what I’ll do when I reread the series (and is what I did when rereading Jim Butcher’s “Dresden Files” series). Either approach is a good one, and will add extra depth to the overall story that would have felt out of place if forced directly into its corresponding novel’s narrative. Anyway, I don’t think it’s a stretch to say that this series has established a new benchmark for space operas and science fiction tales involving space travel. I strongly recommend it for fans of science fiction. Thanks for the recommendation, @Andrzhel, @MrBodyMassageMachineGO, @RapturetheCity Author: James S. A. Corey (nom-de-plume of Daniel Abraham and Ty Franck) Genres: Science fiction, space opera, (occasional) horror Trigger warning: Graphic violence, blood & gore (2011) Leviathan Wakes - 561 pages (2012) Caliban's War - 595 pages (2013) Abaddon's Gate - 539 pages (2014) Cibola Burn - 581 pages (2015) Nemesis Games - 530 pages (2016) Babylon's Ashes - 536 pages (2017) Persepolis Rising - 549 pages (2019) Tiamat's Wrath - 531 pages (2021) Leviathan Falls - 514 pages (2022) Memory's Legion: The Complete Expanse Story Collection - 418 pages
2 “October "Toby" Daye, a changeling who is half human and half fae, has been an outsider from birth. After getting burned by both sides of her heritage, Toby has denied the Faerie world, retreating to a "normal" life. Unfortunately for her, the Faerie world has other ideas... “The murder of Countess Evening Winterrose pulls Toby back into the fae world. Unable to resist Evening's dying curse, which binds her to investigate, Toby must resume her former position as knight errant and renew old alliances. As she steps back into fae society, dealing with a cast of characters not entirely good or evil, she realizes that more than her own life will be forfeited if she cannot find Evening's killer.” I really, really like this series. The further into I got, the faster I was flipping pages! And this may be sacrilege to some, but I’ve come to enjoy this series more than Jim Butcher’s “Dresden Files”, both of which are based on the premise of “city-based P.I. who has magic but is on the ‘outs’ with the local paranormal society gets unavoidably involved with the Irish pantheon and other fae beings”. This is not to trash that series–which is one I adore–but rather to put into context how much I’ve enjoyed this one (and I’m several books behind as of this writing!). ***** SOMEWHAT OF A SPOILER ***** One thing I’ve learned from reading this series is that, when reading fiction, I’m eager to seek permanent solutions (aka, kill the bad guy). There are plenty of times here when I was like “just kill them”, and McGuire sought out an alternative for her story. There’s a lot more forgiveness and considerations for consequences in this series than in most of the fiction I’ve read. It more closely aligns with our behaviors in reality, and I think adds to the overall strength of the series, even if there were times when I was like “things would be a lot easier if character X were to encounter some poetic justice”. It also forced me to reflect on a lot of real-world anger that I carry, and has helped me to cope with frustrations. It doesn’t make them go away (gestures vaguely at the state of America), but it has helped me continue to press forward and retain my sanity in a world run mad. ***** END SPOILER SECTION ***** Thanks for the recommendation, @somnif Author: Seanan McGuire Genres: Urban fantasy, paranormal, noir Trigger warning: Blood. Lots of blood (2009) Rosemary and Rue - 346 pages (2010) A Local Habitation - 377 pages (2010) An Artificial Light - 354 pages (2011) Late Eclipses - 372 pages (2011) One Salt Sea - 354 pages (2012) Ashes of Honor - 353 pages (2013) Chimes at Midnight + short story - 357 pages (2014) The Winter Long - 358 pages (2015) A Rose-Red Chain - 358 pages (2016) Once Broken Faith (includes novella) - 420 pages (2017) The Brightest Fell (includes novella) - 432 pages (2018) Night and Silence (includes novella) - 403 pages (2019) The Unkindest Tide (includes novella) - 403 pages (2020) A Killing Frost (includes novella) - 400 pages (2021) When Sorrows Come (includes novella) - 404 pages
3 “Felix is an Earth soldier, encased in special body armor designed to withstand Earth's most implacable enemy–a bioengineered, insectoid alien horde. But Felix is also equipped with internal mechanisms that enable him, and his fellow soldiers, to survive battle situations that would destroy a man's mind. “This is a remarkable novel of the horror, the courage, and the aftermath of combat--and how the strength of the human spirit can be the greatest armor of all.” Unlike many books that I’ve read, I had never heard of this author before a fellow Imgurian suggested it. And boy was that a great recommendation! I tore through this at a rapid pace. It’s dark and gritty, and left me thinking deeply about the story long after I finished the last page. It isn’t often that the conclusion of a book has me in the depths of philosophical pondering on the meanings of the book, but here we are. If any literature teachers are looking for something different for their students to study, this one might be a good option. Fair warning though, the subject matter may be tough for some, and it’s definitely not one for children. Thanks for the recommendation, @Mewlip Author: John Steakley Genre: Science fiction, military fiction Trigger warning: Graphic violence (1984) Armor - 426 pages
4 “When Rohan became the new prince of the Desert, ruler of the kingdom granted to his family for as long as the Long Sands spewed fire, he took the crown with two goals in mind. First and foremost, he sought to bring permanent peace to his world of divided princedoms. And, in a land where dragon-slaying was a proof of manhood, Rohan was the sole champion of the dragons, fighting desperately to preserve the last remaining lords of the sky and with them a secret which might be the salvation of his people.... “Sioned, the Sunrunner witch who was fated by Fire to be Rohan’s bride, had mastered the magic of sunlight and moonglow, catching hints of a yet to be formed pattern which could irrevocably affect the destinies of Sunrunners and ordinary mortals alike. Yet caught in the machinations of the Lady of Goddess Keep, and of Prince Rohan and his sworn enemy, the treacherously cunning High Prince, could Sioned alter this crucial pattern to protect her lord from the menace of a war that threatened to set the land ablaze?” This is an incredibly well-written book (the first of a trilogy, which itself is the first of 2 trilogies). It is well-balanced, and seamlessly weaves world building, geopolitics, violence, and romance together. Oddly enough, I feel myself needing to clarify romance in a literary context here as *not* being of the pornographic “smut”-type romance, but more of a “love between two characters is so integral to the story that it all falls apart if that were excluded” thing. It’s so good that I have trouble reading it. Which is a weird thing to write, but hang with me for a second as I explain what I mean. I suffer from second-hand emotions quite strongly, which makes awkward comedies, (looking at you, Ben Stiller’s film catalogue), really uncomfortable for me. I also have a tough time with anxiety, and things that trigger it in media are hard to consume, even when they are superb (e.g Robin Hobb’s “Realm of the Elderlings” series, or HBO’s “Deadwood”). Alas for me, love-based relationship drama is one of my most severe real-life triggers for anxiety, and the better constructed a story is which relies on that sort of drama, the more affected by it I am. It’s a long winded way to write out “she’s so good at putting this story together that even though I actively try to disengage myself from the characters I’m still irrevocably drawn to and invested in their relationships and their lives, and I’m stupendously anxious about the whole thing.” I have to exercise restraint when reading this series, because I’m a binge-consumer of media, and my anxiety won’t let me do that with this. So I have to be patient, which I’m not good at. The good news is that I’m absolutely stoked to continue reading the series (currently about 200 pages into book 2) and feel confident in recommending this to others. Thanks for the recommendation, @access Author: Melanie Rawn Genre: Epic fantasy (and perhaps a precursor to the rise of romantasy) Trigger warning: Sexual violence (1988) Dragon Prince - 574 pages
5 "Nimona is an impulsive young shapeshifter with a knack for villainy. Lord Ballister Blackheart is a villain with a vendetta. As sidekick and supervillain, Nimona and Lord Blackheart are about to wreak some serious havoc. Their mission: prove to the kingdom that Sir Ambrosius Goldenloin and his buddies at the Institution of Law Enforcement and Heroics aren't the heroes everyone thinks they are. Explosions will be involved. Science and sharks will be, too. But as small acts of mischief escalate into a vicious battle, Lord Blackheart realizes that Nimona's powers are as murky and mysterious as her past. And her unpredictable wild side might be more dangerous than he is willing to admit." This was great fun! I had watched the movie based on the strong recommendation from my sister, and when I spoke to my friend “Hey, have you seen this new movie, ‘Nimona’? I think you’d like it”, they held up a finger, walked to their bookshelf, and handed me this graphic novel. I love both versions of this story, which is generally tough to pull off, and so I enthusiastically endorse them both. Author: ND Stevenson Genre: Fantasy, graphic novel (2015) Nimona - 256 pages
6 “The once-utopian Chasm City—a doomed human settlement on an otherwise inhospitable planet—has been overrun by a virus known as the Melding Plague, capable of infecting any body, organic or computerized. Now, with the entire city corrupted—from the people to the very buildings they inhabit—only the most wretched sort of existence remains. For security operative Tanner Mirabel, it is the landscape of nightmares through which he searches for a low-life postmortal killer. But the stakes are raised when his search brings him face to face with a centuries-old atrocity that history would rather forget.” The timeline has it as a prequel to “Revelation Space”, and characters and events are referenced in “Redemption Ark”. Even so, this book is not necessary for the “Inhibitor Sequence” series and can be treated as its own entity. However, Reynolds is a talented author who weaves intricate stories, and it’d be a shame to read the “Inhibitor Sequence” series and *not* include this one, too. The “Inhibitor Sequence” series is very broad in its scope and scale, and so “Chasm City” helps flesh out more of the specifics of how things function in human space, and adds what I feel to be significant depth to the whole setting of the surrounding series. It’s a “standalone” story set within the broader universe, but those other stories are definitely better off for this having been a part of it. Thanks for the recommendation, @hariseldonplan, @laura378 Author: Alastair Reynolds Genre: Science fiction (2001) Chasm City - 694 pages
7 *******SPOILER******* **** This is book 2 of the Inhibitor Sequence **** “Late in the twenty-sixth century, the human race has advanced enough to accidentally trigger the Inhibitors - alien killing machines designed to detect intelligent life and destroy it. The only hope for humanity lies in the recovery of a secret cache of doomsday weapons -and a renegade named Clavain who is determined to find them. But other factions want the weapons for their own purposes - and the weapons themselves have another agenda altogether…” I desperately wanted to wrap up the whole “Inhibitor Sequence” series this year, but alas, I did not succeed. This series has been so good to this point that even though I have yet to reach the conclusion, I highly recommend it. Reynolds brings enough hard science to the tale that when he bends and breaks the rules of physics it seems like a natural progression of science and technology, and the effort to suspend disbelief is made easier for the reader. The scope and scale are magnificent, and this should be on the shelves for all science fiction adherents. Thanks for the recommendation, @hariseldonplan, @laura378 Author: Alastair Reynolds Genre: Science fiction Inhibitor Sequence No.2, Revelation Space No.3 (2002) Redemption Ark - 694 pages
8 “When young Alec of Kerry is taken prisoner for a crime he didn’t commit, he is certain that his life is at an end. But one thing he never expected was his cellmate. Spy, rogue, thief, and noble, Seregil of Rhiminee is many things–none of them predictable. And when he offers to take on Alec as his apprentice, things may never be the same for either of them. Soon Alec is traveling roads he never knew existed, toward a war he never suspected was brewing. Before long he and Seregil are embroiled in a sinister plot that runs deeper than either can imagine, and that may cost them far more than their lives if they fail. But fortune is as unpredictable as Alec’s new mentor, and this time there just might be… Luck in the Shadows.” I had a good time with this series. At first I expected it to follow the path of other high fantasy where the protagonists get embroiled in ever larger-scale events, a tendency towards jumping the shark, if you will. This series instead followed a path more accurately described as “how can I make these two men’s lives interesting?” It’s a good thing I’m not an aspiring author because I managed to make that sound drab. That isn’t what I mean. It’s more like, some books in this series have that expected escalation. And some don’t. But the ones that follow things more focused on Alec and Seregil themselves rather than on the realm at large don’t feel like a letdown, a diminution of quality, or content filler between novels. This series is accessible for anyone who enjoys fantasy–either as a veteran of the genre or as someone just starting out. Even better, the two protagonists are bisexuals in a world where that’s pretty much the baseline for everyone, and no one really cares at all what someone’s preference is–it’s accepted at face value, as it should be in real life, with no moral projections in one way or another. In an interview from 2001, she commented, “I will say, however, that Seregil stems largely from how bored I was by the standard fantasy heroes of the day. This is going back more than ten years, mind you. Big, brawny, deadpan sword-slingers who never quite get the girl because they're not worthy, and never get laid except for the occasional two-dimensional prostitute. I liked Sam Gamgee much better, along with the darker, more twisted characters of Moorcock's Elric saga and Gloriana. Le Guin's The Left Hand of Darkness opened another whole vista, too, along with Anne Rice's vampire lads, whom I discovered in mid process. In the end, I think I created Seregil just to see if he'd work -- a gay hero, and a gay character who wasn't tragic, evil, victimized, or a bit player thrown in for color.” (source: https://web.archive.org/web/20080727022714/http://www.strangehorizons.com/2001/20010409/flewelling.shtml) Thanks for the recommendation, @onionfun Author: Lynn Flewelling Genres: High fantasy, LGBTQ+ (1996) Luck in the Shadows - 479 pages (1997) Stalking Darkness - 501 pages (1999) Traitor's Moon - 540 pages (2008) Shadow's Return - 522 pages (2010) The White Road - 385 pages (2012) Casket of Souls - 476 pages (2014) Shards of Time - 409 pages
9 “Millennia ago, a magical disaster caused the fall of a great civilization that spanned a continent, and ended a golden age. Corus today is a world of contending countries, of struggling humans, strange animals and elusive supernatural creatures. Though much has been forgotten, it is still a place of magical powers, and of a few people who are Talented enough to use them. “Even as a child, Alucius showed very strong Talent. He was warned never to reveal it, lest others seek to exploit it. But as he grows to young manhood, Alucius must serve in the Militia like his father before him. When his country is invaded by the slave armies of the Matrial, immortal ruler of a nearby land, Alucius is captured and magically enslaved. “A time of changes has come upon all of the world of Corus. If the evil surrounding the Matrial is not ended, those changes will not be happy ones. Alucius and his Talent have a central role to play.” This one ends far better than it begins, in my opinion. Like several of his other fantasy series, this one jumps around in the timeline, with books 1-3 at the end, 4-6 at the beginning, and 7,8 in the middle. Compared to his other fantasy series, this starts off pretty weak, and is straightforward, almost bludgeoning the reader with his opinion. And by this I mean that it’s the same sentiment presented over and over again with little, if any, variation in its presentation, and is told to the reader directly instead of letting the reader “discover” this through the action and dialogue. Those last two books are the best of the bunch, and resemble his other works of fantasy much more than the first in this series did. If you’re looking for a straightforward fantasy where you’re told what the moral is rather than having to discover it, this is a good bet. If you like when a series has a good ending, this is a good bet. If you’re a completionist who wants to read everything from an author you like, this is a good bet. But if you’re looking for high quality fantasy and are short on time, you’re better off reading his other fantasy series, like the “Spellsong Cycle” (5 books), the “Imager Portfolio” (12 books), or the “Saga of Recluce” (24 books so far). And to be perfectly honest, I’m only judging this so harshly from the standard of other series Modesitt has written, so keep that in mind if you choose to pick this one up. Author: L.E. Modesitt, Jr. Genre: Fantasy (2002) Legacies - 586 pages (2003) Darknesses - 521 pages (2004) Sceptors - 738 pages (2005) Alector's Choice - 620 pages (2006) Cadmian's Choice - 609 pages (2006) Soarer's Choice - 558 pages (2008) The Lord-Protector's Daughter - 292 pages (2011) Lady-Protector - 497 pages
10 “Thousands of years in the future, Earth is a desolate ruin. The first human ship to return in millennia discovers an abandoned wasteland inhabited only by a few degenerate or mutated human outcasts. But among them is a boy of immense native intelligence and determination who is captured, taken in, and educated, and disappears--to grow up to become the force behind a plan to make Earth flower again. He is, if not immortal, at least very long-lived, and he plans to build an independent power base out in the galaxy and force the galactic empire to devote centuries and immense resources to the restoration of the ecology of Earth.” Unless you’re a completionist like me–someone who is going to read everything from a specific author–skip this trilogy. It really isn’t very good, and the ending makes the whole adventure seem like a waste of time. This is frustrating for me because Modesitt is my favorite author, and I’ve had a great time reading his fantasy series. It’s an interesting premise poorly executed, and for some reason Modesitt feels the need to make characters immortal in his science fiction (this is the 3rd time I’ve encountered it so far, having read the first half of his published SF works). This was used to a generally good and interesting effect in “The Hammer of Darkness” and in the “Timegod's World” duology. Here it made everything worse, to the point where if this is the first thing from him you’ve ever read, it’s unlikely you ever gave him a second chance. So, uh, yeah. My advice is to skip this. Author: L.E. Modesitt, Jr. Genre: Science fiction Trigger warning (book 3 only): Sexual violence (1987) Dawn for a Distant Earth - 340 pages (1987) The Silent Warrior - 280 pages (1988) In Endless Twilight - 316 pages
11 “When duty calls, Commander Vimes of the Ankh-Morpork constabulary answers—even if he doesn't want to. Now, he's been invited to attend a royal function as both detective and diplomat. The one role he relishes; the other requires, well, ruby tights. Of course, where cops (even those clad in tights) go, alas, crime follows. An attempted assassination and a theft soon lead to a desperate chase from the low halls of Discworld royalty to the legendary fat mines of Überwald, where lard is found in underground seams along with tusks and teeth and other precious ivory artifacts. It's up to the dauntless Vimes—bothered as usual by a familiar cast of Discworld inhabitants (you know, trolls, dwarfs, werewolves, vampires, and such)—to solve the puzzle of the missing pachyderm. Which, of course, he will . . . after all, solving mysteries is his job.” Sir Terry Pratchett delivers once again! (like it was ever in doubt). Great fantasy, great humor, great fun. That’s what Discworld has been, and always will be, for me. This is the 24th Discworld book and the 5th one featuring the City Watch. There are many valid approaches to reading the Discworld series, and many great visual aids and write-ups on those approaches. I’m a simple human–I’ll read them in publication order, because I know they’re going to be good no matter what, and I don’t want to miss out on any potential subtle references to previous works. Whichever your preference is for tackling Discworld (or even if you haven’t yet begun) you’ll have a fun journey. Shoutout to fellow Terry Pratchett enthusiasts: @DumpTrainWreck9000, @ThishasbeenmyTedTok, @listerenfiend Author: Terry Pratchett Genres: Fantasy, comedy Discworld No.24, City Watch No.5 (1999) The Fifth Elephant - 455 pages
12 “William de Worde is the accidental editor of the Discworld's first newspaper. Now he must cope with the traditional perils of a journalist's life - people who want him dead, a recovering vampire with a suicidal fascination for flash photography, some more people who want him dead in a different way and, worst of all, the man who keeps begging him to publish pictures of his humorously shaped potatoes. “William just wants to get at THE TRUTH. Unfortunately, everyone else wants to get at William. And it's only the third edition…” This is the 25th Discworld book and the 2nd one featuring the Industrial Revolution. With the first one, “Moving Pictures” (1990), I wasn’t entirely certain what to expect. Now that I have more experience both with Discworld and with this second book of the Industrial Revolution, I have a greater appreciation for Pratchett’s mastery of satire. It was already pretty high, but now it’s even higher, like finding out there’s one more flight to the staircase you weren’t expecting. Except, you know, a fun version of that. The books in this series lampoon a specific element of industry that was pivotal in Earth society, things which revolutionized how we interact with the world. In “Moving Pictures” it was cinema and visual entertainment. In “The Truth” it’s the printing press and news media. I’m stoked to learn what elements from our society will wreak havoc in future installments of the Industrial Revolution in Discworld. Whatever the thing will be* I know it’ll be a blast. Shoutout to fellow Terry Pratchett enthusiasts: @DumpTrainWreck9000, @ThishasbeenmyTedTok, @listerenfiend Author: Terry Pratchett Genres: Fantasy, comedy Discworld No.25, Industrial Revolution No.2 (2000) The Truth - 435 pages *Look, I know they were already published ages ago, but I don’t like spoiling it for myself by checking it out before I’ve read that far.
13 “Hegira is a huge, unmapped planet, home to hundreds of tribes and dozens of exotic cities. And scattered across Hegira are giant monoliths, holding all the knowledge humans brought to Hegira. In a stunning coming-of-age story, a young man and his two companions travel Hegira on a journey of discovery.” This was an interesting read, though I admit to being largely confused by it when I got to the end. I think some folks have described this as suffering from “first novel syndrome”, where the ideas and setting have great promise, but the execution is substandard or incomplete. I’d agree on that point, unless there’s some nuance that I completely missed. Nevertheless, I enjoyed reading it and look forward to seeing how Bear grew as an author. Fun fact (which I did not know until compiling this post) Greg Bear is one of the founders of San Diego Comic-Con! Thanks for the recommendation, @MissivesFromTheTower Author: Greg Bear Genre: Science fiction (1979) Hegira - 240 pages
14 “Desperate to defend his land from advancing hordes of bestial charr, King Adelbern summoned the all-powerful Foefire to repel the invaders. But magic can be a double-edged sword—the Foefire burned both charr and human alike. While the charr corpses smoldered, the slain Ascalonians arose again, transformed by their king’s rage into ghostly protectors and charged with guarding the realm . . . forever. The once mighty kingdom became a haunted shadow of its former glory. “Centuries later, the descendants of Ascalon, exiled to the nation of Kryta, are besieged on all sides. To save humankind, Queen Jennah seeks to negotiate a treaty with the hated charr. But one obstacle remains. The charr legions won’t sign the truce until their most prized possession, the Claw of the Khan-Ur, is returned from the ruins of fallen Ascalon. “Now a mismatched band of adventurers, each plagued by ghosts of their own, sets forth into a haunted, war-torn land to retrieve the Claw. Without the artifact, there is no hope for peace between human and charr—but the undead king who rules Ascalon won’t give it up easily, and not everyone wants peace!” As someone who has been playing the Guild Wars franchise since 2004, I was supremely nervous about reading these books. In fact, I would have avoided them altogether (like I’ve done with other novelizations of video games) if I hadn’t read Drew Karpyshyn’s “Mass Effect” books, where I learned that sometimes excellent stories and context don’t have a place in the games themselves, and are more suited to a literary environment. For some reason, I thought that my enjoyment of the games would be lessened by the books, like my past would somehow be tarnished or the fun sapped. What a weird thought to have and let take hold. I’m so glad I’ve moved past that mentality, because these Guild Wars books are perfect for the Guild Wars gamer. These three books, all written by different authors, provide additional context that bridges the gap between the original game franchise (Prophecies, Factions, Nightfall, Eye of the North) and the release of Guild Wars 2 in 2012. Whether or not ArenaNet were committed to launching a whole new game series at the time “Ghosts of Ascalon” and “Edge of Destiny” were written, or if they used them as feelers to see if it was worthwhile is something I don’t have any knowledge on. I’m just glad to have them. The interesting thing about this is that these were published in reverse chronological order with respect to each other. In fact, this is one of the rare times when my recommendation is for these to be read in internal chronological order rather than publication order, (so, “Sea of Sorrows” → “Edge of Destiny” → “Ghosts of Ascalon”), especially if the reader has not played the Guild Wars 2 franchise. While these will be most enjoyable for folks who have experience with the games because they are all prequels to GW2 and provide interesting additional context to the world, the stories are fun in and of themselves. You don’t have to have played the game, or be a gamer at all, in order to enjoy these books. Authors: Matt Forbeck and Jeff Grubb | J. Robert King | Ree Soesbee Genre: Fantasy, MMORPG (2009) Guild Wars: Ghosts of Ascalon - 370 pages (2010) Guild Wars: Edge of Destiny - 413 pages (2013) Guild Wars: Sea of Sorrows - 478 pages
15 “Meet Maimun—an orphan who couldn’t imagine how unfortunate it would be to discover a stone that makes him forever lucky. Fleeing a powerful demon named Asbeel, Maimun stows away aboard the ship Sea Sprite , where he encounters a cast of characters well-known to Salvatore’s readers: Captain Deudermont, Drizzt Do’Urden, Wulfgar, Catti-brie, and the dwarf Bruenor. Drizzt becomes a mentor to Maimun and aboard Deudermont’s ship, they sail the treacherous seas of the Forgotten Realms. Hunted by a demon, haunted by a mysterious spellcaster, and chased by a pirate, Maimun, with Drizzt’s help, must unravel the secrets of the Stone of Tymora before his luck runs out!” This is a trilogy of young-adult novels (though I’d consider them more like novellas) centered upon Maimun, written in partnership with his son, Geno. Even though the blurb above uses Drizzt to help sell the book, he does not feature that often in the story. We were first introduced to Maimun as a character in the Drizzt story “The Pirate King” (2008), though this trilogy has them crossing paths way back in the early days of the Drizzt series, around about the time of “The Halfling’s Gem” (1990). This was a fun read, and it was nice to step back in theme a bit, as the preceding Salvatore works in the “Legend of Drizzt” series had taken a darker tone. The good thing about this is that even though this is very much tied into the Forgotten Realms setting, you don’t need to read any of the other books to pick this one up. Yes, we’ll encounter Drizzt, but knowledge of who he is and what he and his companions have achieved are not critical to the story. Note: Attempting to get paperback copies of the three books individually is prohibitively expensive for some reason, but the omnibus version was appropriately priced. If you’re looking for it in print, that’s the option I recommend. Shoutout to fellow R.A. Salvatore enthusiasts: @SilvanOrion, @theearbiter Authors: R.A. Salvatore & Geno Salvatore Genre: High fantasy (2012) Stone of Tymora - 344 pages Side note regarding the image: This is the original creation of the cover artist, Tyler Jacobson, who did the artwork for the omnibus edition of the trilogy. I couldn’t find versions with the title & author that were of anywhere near decent quality, so I pulled this image from his blog (which I learned yesterday I can’t include the URL here directly without triggering Imgur’s auto-block spam filter)
16 “Sean Carroll turns his attention to the deepest question in physics. Already hailed as a masterpiece, ‘Something Deeply Hidden’ shows for the first time that facing up to the essential puzzle of quantum mechanics utterly transforms how we think about space and time. The proposal that our universe emerges from quantum entanglement makes this book eye-opening, mind-bending, essential reading. “The Many-Worlds theory of quantum behavior says that every time there is a quantum event, a world splits off that is exactly the same, except in that other world the quantum event didn’t happen. And so, thousands of copies of you are generated every second. Rarely does a book so fully reorganize how we think about our place in the universe.” To quote from his prologue, “Don’t Be Afraid”: “This book has three main messages. The first is that quantum mechanics should be understandable–even if we’re not there yet–and achieving such understanding should be a high-priority goal of modern science [...] The second message is that we have made real progress toward understanding. [...] The third message is that all this matters, and not just for the integrity of science.” Sean Carroll is a strong proponent of the Many-Worlds theory of quantum mechanics, and so it is not surprising that this is the foundation of the book. He lays it all out in a logical fashion, working diligently to present the facts, and walks us through why Many-Worlds is the most logical description of our observations. Importantly, he does this without bashing competing theories, and endeavors to remove emotion from the picture altogether (even though the history of quantum mechanical theories includes emotionally charged confrontations and histrionics). He also dedicates a small portion of the book to explain what the other leading alternatives to Many-Worlds are in an unbiased and straightforward manner, and emphasizes that while Many-Worlds is what makes the most sense to him, it’s up to each reader to determine if they agree or not. I always enjoy reading his books, and look forward to reading his next two books. He’s also an engaging speaker. No matter the medium, he makes an immensely complicated subject imminently accessible to the educated layperson (e.g. someone who comprehends basic science but who doesn’t specialize in physics). If reading about quantum physics isn’t your cup of tea, but would still be interested in learning more, Sean Carroll has done a number of lectures targeted to the general public, and are worth watching. I’ve linked a few below from the Royal Institution’s YouTube channel: “The Particle at the End of the Universe” [58:07] (2013) https://youtu.be/RwdY7Eqyguo?si=MNnGB67svp-tTwlu “A Brief History of Quantum Mechanics” [56:11] (2020) https://youtu.be/5hVmeOCJjOU?si=14SPYo65evVd_DaZ “The Quantum Revolution” [56:17] (2024) https://youtu.be/dCrbOmBsTRk?si=hHqFPrODCeE9_Eqr Author: Sean Carroll Genres: Science, quantum physics (2019) Something Deeply Hidden: Quantum Worlds and the Emergence of Spacetime - 321 pages
17 “Fans of xkcd ask Munroe a lot of strange questions: What if you tried to hit a baseball pitched at 90 percent the speed of light? How fast can you hit a speed bump while driving and live? If there was a robot apocalypse, how long would humanity last? What if everyone only had one soulmate? What would happen if the moon went away? “In pursuit of answers, Munroe runs computer simulations, pores over stacks of declassified military research memos, solves differential equations, and consults with nuclear reactor operators. His responses are masterpieces of clarity and hilarity, complemented by his signature xkcd comics. (They often predict the complete annihilation of humankind, or at least a really big explosion.)” Honestly, the blurb there says it better than any endorsement I could offer. If you like xkcd and like to learn, you’ll love this. If you’ve never encountered xkcd before, you’ll still love this (confirmed by my mother who has never seen anything from Munroe, and thoroughly enjoyed the audiobook version of this). Author: Randall Munroe Genre: Science (2014) What If? Serious Scientific Answers to Absurd Hypothetical Questions - 295 pages
18 Butterfly tax! I took this photo in the summer, and the fine folks on iNaturalist have identified it as a Tawny-edged Skipper (Polites themistocles). *** This was my 2024 reading bonanza. I’m hopeful that y’all find something new to read from this list, or perhaps have bumped an existing item higher up on your own list. My shelves are overfull to the point of double stacking, but I won’t let that slow me down. MOAR BOOKS PLEASE! 2023's shelf additions: https://imgur.com/gallery/year-of-over-filling-shelves-y4t4bsF 2024's shelf additions: [gallery to be compiled] *** Books read this year: 57 Average page count: 445.86 *** 2019's list: https://imgur.com/gallery/TeAUSBl 2020's list: https://imgur.com/gallery/9z7qRni 2021's list: https://imgur.com/gallery/Llmsioi 2022’s list: https://imgur.com/gallery/sxGlaZn 2023’s list: https://imgur.com/gallery/I7W27C9

item.title: Syanna Cosplay - The Witcher 3
item.description:
First cosplay of 2025! Syanna from the Witcher 3. Been waiting to do this one for way too long, but also kinda needed the weather where it is so I could complete "the vision". Maybe I'll post up a bts showing how I did this set! I will have time for that now... 2025 means I get to start this as my job full time, and I cannot wait!
Character reference

item.title: I DID THE THING!!!!
item.description: It took them almost a full month to tell me. I have been waiting for soooooo long for this email. Finally graduated! God I need a break from writing papers but I now need to work on a masters. Whatever, I'll think about that later, today I am just happy that I am finally a college graduate. Only took me til I was 36, but hey better late than never huh?
